title: Accept int% and float-fraction; teach value errors
summary: Auto-detect 0-100 int and 0.0-1.0 float for --related-min-relevance, normalize internally, and replace raw Go ParseInt error with a structured pedagogical message that names both accepted units. Eliminates silent first-try failure when an agent passes 0.5 by analogy to sibling --relations-threshold.
risk: Backward-compatible for existing int callers (0..100). Float values that previously errored now succeed — strict scripts that grep stderr for 'invalid syntax' on 0.5 would change behavior, but that path was a hard error, not a documented contract. No deprecation needed; help-text bump is sufficient. Risk of agents passing 50.0 (ambiguous: 50 percent or 5000% as float) — disambiguated by the 0.0-1.0 range check (50.0 → 'out of range; try 50 for integer form').
test_plan: Pattern: golden-error + accept-both-units round-trip. Pin (1) bv --robot-related --related-min-relevance 20 → exit 0; (2) bv --robot-related --related-min-relevance 0.20 → exit 0 with same effective filter as (1); (3) bv --robot-related --related-min-relevance 0.5 → exit 0 (was: silent ParseInt fail); (4) bv --robot-related --related-min-relevance abc 2>&1 → contains 'expected integer 0-100' AND 'float 0.0-1.0'; (5) bv --robot-related --related-min-relevance 150 2>&1 → contains 'out of range' AND mentions sibling flag; exit 2. Tests under audit/regression_tests/R-NNN__related_min_relevance_units.test.sh.
anchor_pattern: Pattern-6 from CANONICAL-EXEMPLARS (stable structured error output) + units-normalization extension
counter_example: CE-silent-unit-mismatch (sibling flags with divergent numeric units; one accepts only int, the other only float, both labeled as 'relevance/threshold')

title: Levenshtein-1 typo correction for --suggest-bead and siblings
summary: When an agent types a near-miss like '--sugges-bead' or '--suggest-bd', the global unknown-flag handler emits a bare 'unknown flag' error with no hint. Add Pattern-1 Levenshtein-1 typo correction so the error names the closest valid flag (e.g. '--suggest-bead'). Compute the candidate list at build time from the clap metadata so it stays in sync with --robot-suggest's actual filter flags.
risk: Low. The hint is additive on stderr; existing scripts grepping for 'unknown flag' still match. The exit code stays 2. Risk vector: a future flag rename could create a Levenshtein-1 collision (e.g. --suggest-bead vs --suggest-head) and yield a misleading hint — mitigated by the build-time generation (no stale hardcoded list) and by suggesting only when the candidate is unique at distance 1. No deprecation path needed (this is purely additive).
test_plan: Pattern from REGRESSION-TEST-PATTERNS.md: shell-level golden test (Pattern-1's own template). Pin: 'bv --sugges-bead foo 2>&1' contains the substring "did you mean '--suggest-bead'" AND exit code is 2 AND stdout is empty. Second assertion: 'bv --completely-unrelated-flag 2>&1' does NOT contain 'did you mean' (no false-positive at distance > 1). Place at audit/regression_tests/R-NNN__suggest_bead_typo.test.sh.
anchor_pattern: Pattern 1 from CANONICAL-EXEMPLARS (typo-correcting unknown-flag handler with did-you-mean)
counter_example: CE-1 from COUNTER-EXAMPLES (bare 'unknown flag' with no Levenshtein hint)

title: Add Levenshtein-1 typo correction for bv unknown flags
summary: When bv encounters an unknown flag like --hlep, suggest the closest known flag (e.g. --help) at edit-distance 1. Today bv prints a bare "unknown flag" without any did-you-mean hint, so agents cannot self-correct typos without re-reading --help.
risk: Low. Hint is additive on stderr; existing scripts that grep stderr for 'unknown flag' still match the literal substring (we keep that wording as the leading clause). Distance-1 cap prevents false-positive suggestions for genuinely-unrelated typos. No flag aliases are added, so no surface contract changes.
test_plan: Pattern-1 regression test from REC-PATTERNS.md: a shell script under audit/regression_tests/ that invokes 'bv --hlep' with stdin=/dev/null, asserts stderr contains "did you mean '--help'" and exit code is 2; a second case asserts 'bv --robo-triage' suggests '--robot-triage'; a third asserts a far-off typo ('bv --xyzzy') falls back to the generic message with no did-you-mean line. Pins both the hint format and the no-false-positive guarantee.
counter_example: CE-1 (useless_error: bare 'unknown flag' with no suggestion)
